title: How Do Large Language Monkeys Get Their Power (Laws)?

reviews.summary: The paper investigates why, across diverse tasks, the negative log of the average success rate under repeated sampling (k attempts per item) follows a power law in k, despite the fact that for any single problem, the failure probability should drop exponentially with k. The authors resolve this apparent paradox by showing that aggregate power-law behavior arises when the distribution of single-attempt success probabilities across problems has a heavy left tail, specifically p(pass@1) ∝ p^{b−1} near zero, and they prove sufficiency and (under mild regularity) necessity results via Laplace/Tauberian-style arguments. Building on this distributional perspective, they propose a practical estimator that fits the single-attempt success distribution and simulates aggregate scaling, demonstrating improved sample efficiency over least-squares fits on synthetic and real datasets, and explaining deviations from power-law scaling in prior work.
strengths: Establishes a clear and rigorous link between per-problem exponential decay and aggregate power-law decay via the left tail of the across-problem single-attempt success distribution.
Provides both sufficiency and (conditional) necessity theorems, leveraging classical Laplace/Tauberian reasoning to characterize when power-law scaling must arise.

Introduces a practically useful “distributional estimator” that fits the single-attempt success distribution (accounting for limited resolution in the extreme left tail) and predicts scaling parameters with notably improved sample efficiency.
Confirms the per-problem exponential scaling empirically on two domains: math problem solving (Pythia on MATH) and multimodal jailbreaking (frontier models on HarmBench).

Demonstrates that observed deviations from power-law behavior (e.g., Llama 3 8B IT in Best-of-N jailbreaking) correspond to an absence of the required heavy left tail in pass@1, aligning with the theory.
Provides backtesting with synthetic data to quantify the estimator’s relative-error advantages over least squares.
Offers a compelling conceptual narrative that reconciles exponential per-item improvements with aggregate power-law behavior; figures reinforce the intuition.
Theorems are stated cleanly with clear claims about the behavior of p(pass@1) near zero and the resulting scaling.
Practical implications and forecasting procedures are well-motivated.
Unifies several empirical observations of “inference-time scaling” under repeated sampling, clarifying when and why power laws should emerge.
Provides a predictive, more compute-efficient methodology with immediate utility for evaluation design and compute planning.
Suggests broader connections to pretraining scaling via mixtures of decays, potentially informing how and when power laws emerge in other regimes.
weaknesses: Independence of attempts is assumed; in practice, repeated sampling can induce correlations (e.g., shared prompts, decoding heuristics, or adaptive strategies). The impact of correlations on the exponential per-problem decay and on the distributional estimator is not analyzed.

Necessity result likely relies on regular variation and Tauberian conditions; the paper references “mild regularity” but the precise conditions and their practical testability are not detailed in the main text.
Real-data studies use relatively small benchmarks (128 MATH problems; 159 HarmBench prompts), which may limit the reliability of inferred left-tail behavior and exponent estimates.

Goodness-of-fit for the proposed distribution families (e.g., scaled Kumaraswamy-/Beta-Binomial) is shown visually; quantitative tests (KS, Cramér–von Mises, likelihood ratios, information criteria) are not reported.

Claims of “2–4 orders-of-magnitude less inference compute” rest primarily on synthetic backtesting; a concrete operational definition of “inference compute” and a broader set of real-data backtests would strengthen the claim.

Some inconsistencies/typos in naming fitted distributions (e.g., references to scaled Beta-Binomial vs. scaled Kumaraswamy; notational artifacts like “pass;@1”) make it harder to track exactly what is fit where.
The statistical estimation details (discretization procedure, parameter constraints, optimization) are only sketched; more precise description would help reproducibility.

The mixture-of-exponentials-to-power-law connection is classical; citing additional standard sources from regular variation/Tauberian theory (e.g., Feller, Bingham–Goldie–Teugels) would contextualize the necessity/sufficiency claims.

Connections to randomized algorithm runtimes and restart strategies under heavy-tailed distributions (e.g., Luby et al.-style analyses) could further situate the findings relative to broader CS theory on “best-of-N”/amplification phenomena.
reviews.questions:
1. How sensitive are the main conclusions and the distributional estimator to correlations across attempts (e.g., shared prompts, reranking, or adaptive sampling)? Can you characterize conditions under which mild correlations preserve the effective exponential per-problem decay?
2. Could you provide quantitative goodness-of-fit metrics (e.g., KS statistics, AIC/BIC) for the fitted pass@1 distributions across models/benchmarks, and compare alternative families beyond scaled Kumaraswamy/Beta-Binomial?
3. What precise regularity assumptions are required for the necessity result, and can you reference or restate the applicable Tauberian theorem(s) to make the proof self-contained?
4. In real-data backtesting, how does the distributional estimator perform at reduced sampling budgets compared to least squares (e.g., halving the number of attempts or problems)? Can you report confidence intervals for the estimated exponent b?
5. How do decoding parameters (temperature, nucleus sampling p, presence/absence of chain-of-thought) influence the inferred pass@1 distribution and the resulting exponent? Is the tail exponent stable across these choices?
6. Can the method generalize to other domains with pass@k metrics (e.g., code generation/HumanEval, GSM8K variants, theorem proving)? Any preliminary results or negative cases?
7. In safety settings, can your framework help choose a budget k that bounds residual attack success at a desired confidence, given uncertainty about the left tail (e.g., via conservative tail modeling)?
reviews.assessment:
This paper offers a clear and conceptually satisfying resolution of an important empirical puzzle: why aggregate success under repeated sampling follows a power law even though each problem improves exponentially with attempts. The distributional mechanism and accompanying theorems are technically sound, leveraging established asymptotics, and the empirical analyses convincingly support the key claims across two application areas. The proposed distributional estimator is a practical contribution with demonstrated sample-efficiency benefits in backtesting and plausible utility for forecasting.

While some presentation details (distribution nomenclature, estimation procedure) could be sharpened and the empirical section would benefit from additional quantitative fits, robustness checks, and real-data backtesting at reduced budgets, these are addressable concerns. The work is timely, broadly relevant to inference-time scaling and evaluation methodology, and likely to be valuable to both practitioners and researchers. I recommend acceptance.
